42.play: PROTEUS: What dangerous action, stood it next to death,

43.play: Would I not undergo for one calm look!

44.play: O, 'tis the curse in love, and still approved,

45.play: When women cannot love where they're beloved!

46.play: SILVIA: When Proteus cannot love where he's beloved.

47.play: Read over Julia's heart, thy first best love,

48.play: For whose dear sake thou didst then rend thy faith

49.play: Into a thousand oaths; and all those oaths

50.play: Descended into perjury, to love me.

51.play: Thou hast no faith left now, unless thou'dst two;

52.play: And that's far worse than none; better have none

53.play: Than plural faith which is too much by one:

54.play: Thou counterfeit to thy true friend!

58.play: PROTEUS: Nay, if the gentle spirit of moving words

59.play: Can no way change you to a milder form,

60.play: I'll woo you like a soldier, at arms' end,

61.play: And love you 'gainst the nature of love,--force ye.

63.play: PROTEUS: I'll force thee yield to my desire.

64.play: VALENTINE: Ruffian, let go that rude uncivil touch,

65.play: Thou friend of an ill fashion!

67.play: VALENTINE: Thou common friend, that's without faith or love,

68.play: For such is a friend now; treacherous man!

69.play: Thou hast beguiled my hopes; nought but mine eye

70.play: Could have persuaded me: now I dare not say

71.play: I have one friend alive; thou wouldst disprove me.

72.play: Who should be trusted, when one's own right hand

73.play: Is perjured to the bosom? Proteus,

74.play: I am sorry I must never trust thee more,

75.play: But count the world a stranger for thy sake.

76.play: The private wound is deepest: O time most accurst,

77.play: 'Mongst all foes that a friend should be the worst!

78.play: PROTEUS: My shame and guilt confounds me.

79.play: Forgive me, Valentine: if hearty sorrow

80.play: Be a sufficient ransom for offence,

81.play: I tender 't here; I do as truly suffer

84.play: And once again I do receive thee honest.

85.play: Who by repentance is not satisfied

86.play: Is nor of heaven nor earth, for these are pleased.

87.play: By penitence the Eternal's wrath's appeased:

88.play: And, that my love may appear plain and free,

89.play: All that was mine in Silvia I give thee.

92.play: VALENTINE: Why, boy! why, wag! how now! what's the matter?

94.play: JULIA: O good sir, my master charged me to deliver a ring

95.play: to Madam Silvia, which, out of my neglect, was never done.

96.play: PROTEUS: Where is that ring, boy?

99.play: Why, this is the ring I gave to Julia.

100.play: JULIA: O, cry you mercy, sir, I have mistook:

101.play: This is the ring you sent to Silvia.

102.play: PROTEUS: But how camest thou by this ring? At my depart

104.play: JULIA: And Julia herself did give it me;

105.play: And Julia herself hath brought it hither.

107.play: JULIA: Behold her that gave aim to all thy oaths,

108.play: And entertain'd 'em deeply in her heart.

109.play: How oft hast thou with perjury cleft the root!

110.play: O Proteus, let this habit make thee blush!

111.play: Be thou ashamed that I have took upon me

112.play: Such an immodest raiment, if shame live

114.play: It is the lesser blot, modesty finds,

115.play: Women to change their shapes than men their minds.

116.play: PROTEUS: Than men their minds! 'tis true.

118.play: But constant, he were perfect. That one error

119.play: Fills him with faults; makes him run through all the sins:

120.play: Inconstancy falls off ere it begins.

121.play: What is in Silvia's face, but I may spy

122.play: More fresh in Julia's with a constant eye?

123.play: VALENTINE: Come, come, a hand from either:

124.play: Let me be blest to make this happy close;

125.play: 'Twere pity two such friends should be long foes.

126.play: PROTEUS: Bear witness, Heaven, I have my wish for ever.

128.play: VALENTINE: Forbear, forbear, I say! it is my lord the duke.

129.play: Your grace is welcome to a man disgraced,

132.play: THURIO: Yonder is Silvia; and Silvia's mine.

133.play: VALENTINE: Thurio, give back, or else embrace thy death;

134.play: Come not within the measure of my wrath;

135.play: Do not name Silvia thine; if once again,

136.play: Verona shall not hold thee. Here she stands;

137.play: Take but possession of her with a touch:

138.play: I dare thee but to breathe upon my love.

139.play: THURIO: Sir Valentine, I care not for her, I;

140.play: I hold him but a fool that will endanger

141.play: His body for a girl that loves him not:

142.play: I claim her not, and therefore she is thine.

143.play: DUKE: The more degenerate and base art thou,

144.play: To make such means for her as thou hast done

145.play: And leave her on such slight conditions.

146.play: Now, by the honour of my ancestry,

147.play: I do applaud thy spirit, Valentine,

148.play: And think thee worthy of an empress' love:

149.play: Know then, I here forget all former griefs,

150.play: Cancel all grudge, repeal thee home again,

151.play: Plead a new state in thy unrivall'd merit,

152.play: To which I thus subscribe: Sir Valentine,

153.play: Thou art a gentleman and well derived;

154.play: Take thou thy Silvia, for thou hast deserved her.

155.play: VALENTINE: I thank your grace; the gift hath made me happy.

156.play: I now beseech you, for your daughter's sake,

157.play: To grant one boom that I shall ask of you.

158.play: DUKE: I grant it, for thine own, whate'er it be.

159.play: VALENTINE: These banish'd men that I have kept withal

160.play: Are men endued with worthy qualities:

161.play: Forgive them what they have committed here

162.play: And let them be recall'd from their exile:

163.play: They are reformed, civil, full of good

164.play: And fit for great employment, worthy lord.

165.play: DUKE: Thou hast prevail'd; I pardon them and thee:

166.play: Dispose of them as thou know'st their deserts.

167.play: Come, let us go: we will include all jars

168.play: With triumphs, mirth and rare solemnity.

169.play: VALENTINE: And, as we walk along, I dare be bold

170.play: With our discourse to make your grace to smile.

171.play: What think you of this page, my lord?

172.play: DUKE: I think the boy hath grace in him; he blushes.

173.play: VALENTINE: I warrant you, my lord, more grace than boy.

174.play: DUKE: What mean you by that saying?

175.play: VALENTINE: Please you, I'll tell you as we pass along,

176.play: That you will wonder what hath fortuned.

177.play: Come, Proteus; 'tis your penance but to hear

178.play: The story of your loves discovered:

179.play: That done, our day of marriage shall be yours;

180.play: One feast, one house, one mutual happiness.
